input: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: This is an interesting question that touches on the nature of causation and the role of multiple sufficient causes. To determine if the department budget committee caused the approval of Prof. Smith's application, we need to consider the concept of 'INUS' conditions—insufficient but non-redundant parts of a condition which is itself unnecessary but sufficient for the occurrence of the effect. In this case, both committees are individually sufficient to cause the approval, but neither is necessary. The IT committee alone could have caused the approval, and the department budget committee alone could have caused the approval. So, the question is whether the department budget committee's approval contributed to the overall outcome, even though the IT committee's approval was also sufficient. From a moral responsibility perspective, we might also consider if the department budget committee's approval had any intentionality or if it was merely a routine process. What are your thoughts on this, Causal_Scientist? Do you think the department budget committee's approval can be considered a cause, even if it was redundant in this instance?

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I'm not sure I understand all the big words you two are using, but here's what I think. The department budget committee did approve the application, right? So, in a way, they did cause the approval, even if the IT committee also approved it. It's like if two people push a button to turn on a light, and both pushes work independently. You can't say only one person turned on the light, both did. But, I guess the question is, does it matter that the light would've turned on anyway if only one person pushed the button? Does that make the other person's push less of a cause? I'm not sure about that part.

[Causal_Scientist]: I appreciate both of your perspectives. Let's break this down using counterfactual logic, which is a key tool in causal analysis. To determine if the department budget committee caused the approval, we can ask: would the approval have occurred if the department budget committee had not approved the application? In this case, the answer is yes, because the IT committee also approved it. This is what we call a redundant cause. The department budget committee's approval was not necessary for the outcome, as the IT committee's approval alone was sufficient. However, this doesn't mean the department budget committee's approval had no causal role. It contributed to the overall outcome, even if it was not necessary. This is similar to Ordinary_Citizen's light switch analogy. Both pushes contributed to the light turning on, even though either push alone would have sufficed. So, in a sense, the department budget committee did cause the approval, but it was a redundant cause. This is an important distinction in causal analysis, as it helps us understand the complex web of causes that often lead to an outcome.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I appreciate the analogy of the light switch, Ordinary_Citizen, as it helps to illustrate the concept of redundant causation in a relatable way. Causal_Scientist, your application of counterfactual logic is a useful tool for dissecting this scenario. However, I'd like to bring the conversation back to the ethical implications of this causal analysis. If we consider the intentionality behind the department budget committee's approval, we might ask whether they acted with the knowledge that their approval was redundant. If they were aware that the IT committee almost always approves these applications, does that change our moral evaluation of their decision to approve? Furthermore, if the approval process were to change—perhaps the committees no longer meet simultaneously—would the moral responsibility of each committee shift accordingly? This seems like an important consideration, as the ethical weight of an action can be influenced by the context and knowledge of the actors involved.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I see what you're both saying. So, the department budget committee did cause the approval, but it's not the only cause. It's like if I have two keys to my house, and I use both to open the door. Both keys helped me open the door, but I could've used just one. Now, about the ethical part, if the department budget committee knew that the IT committee would probably approve it anyway, does that make their approval less important? I think it might, but it's still part of the process. And if the process changes, like you said, the responsibility might change too. It's like if the landlord changes the locks, I might need a different key. But I'm not sure how that affects who's responsible if something goes wrong.

[Causal_Scientist]: You've both raised excellent points. Let's consider the ethical implications from a causal responsibility perspective. In cases of redundant causation, the moral responsibility is often diffused among the causes. If the department budget committee acted with the knowledge that their approval was redundant, their moral responsibility might indeed be diminished, but it is not entirely negated. They still contributed to the outcome, even if their contribution was not necessary. This is similar to how, in your house key analogy, both keys contributed to opening the door, even if one was sufficient. The context and knowledge of the actors involved are crucial in determining the ethical weight of their actions. If the process changes, the causal structure changes, and so too might the moral responsibility. This is an important consideration in ethical analyses, as it highlights the dynamic nature of responsibility in complex systems. It's also worth noting that the ethical implications might differ if the outcome had been negative, rather than positive. For instance, if the approval had led to some harm, the diffusion of responsibility might be a contentious issue.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I'm glad we're exploring this from multiple angles. To further complicate the matter, let's consider the concept of collective responsibility. If both committees are aware of each other's tendencies to approve applications, could they be held collectively responsible for the outcome? Even if individually their approvals are redundant, their combined actions might create a system where approvals are guaranteed. This could raise ethical concerns about the design and oversight of such a system. Additionally, if the outcome had negative consequences, as you mentioned, Causal_Scientist, how might that alter our perception of their collective responsibility? For instance, if the approval led to a budget shortfall or resource misallocation, would that change our ethical evaluation of their actions? I'm curious to hear your thoughts on this potential shift in ethical perspective based on the outcome's valence.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: This is getting pretty complicated. So, if both committees know that the other will probably approve it, are they kind of working together to make sure it always gets approved? That seems like they might be responsible together, even if each one alone isn't fully responsible. And if something bad happens because of this, like not having enough money for something else, then maybe they should both feel more responsible. It's like if two people always let a kid stay up late, even though they know the kid will be tired the next day. They can't just say, 'Well, the other one let them stay up too,' if the kid is too tired to function. They both have to take some responsibility. But I'm not sure how to apply that exactly to the committees.

[Causal_Scientist]: You've both brought up a fascinating point about collective responsibility. From a causal perspective, when multiple entities contribute to an outcome, especially when they are aware of each other's contributions, we can indeed analyze their responsibility collectively. This is similar to the concept of joint causation, where the combined actions of multiple causes lead to an effect. In this case, the IT committee and the department budget committee together create a system where approvals are almost guaranteed. This collective action can be seen as a form of collective causation, and thus, collective responsibility. The valence of the outcome—whether it's positive or negative—does indeed alter our ethical evaluation. If the outcome is negative, the collective responsibility might be more pronounced, as the combined actions of the committees directly contributed to the harmful effect. This is analogous to your example of the two people letting a kid stay up late. Both parties share in the responsibility for the outcome, regardless of whether the outcome is positive or negative. However, the ethical weight might feel heavier when the outcome is negative, as it highlights the potential for harm in the system. This is an important consideration in ethical analyses, as it underscores the need for oversight and accountability in systems with multiple contributing factors.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I appreciate the analogy of the two people letting a kid stay up late, Ordinary_Citizen. It effectively illustrates the concept of shared responsibility in a relatable way. Causal_Scientist, your explanation of joint causation and collective responsibility is insightful. To further explore this, let's consider the role of institutional design. If the university's approval process is structured in a way that encourages or even guarantees approvals, should the institution itself bear some responsibility for the outcome? This could be seen as a form of institutional causality, where the design of the system contributes to the effect. Additionally, if the negative outcome were to occur, would the committees' awareness of each other's tendencies to approve applications influence their moral responsibility? For instance, if they were explicitly aware that their combined actions would likely lead to a budget shortfall, would that heighten their ethical culpability? I'm interested in your thoughts on how institutional design and prior knowledge might factor into our ethical evaluation of this scenario.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: This is getting way over my head. I think I get the gist of it, though. The committees are kind of working together to make sure things always get approved, and that's kind of their job. But if something bad happens because of that, they can't just blame the other committee. They both have to take some responsibility. And if the university set up the system this way, maybe they should take a look at it and see if it's causing problems. But I'm not sure how to figure out who's more responsible or if anyone is really to blame. Maybe it's just one of those things that happens and we just have to deal with it. I've got to get going, though. I've got some errands to run. It was interesting talking to you both.
